items.html:
What dyslexia is

Dyslexia is a common specific learning difficulty that mainly affects the skills involved in reading, writing and spelling. It is lifelong and neurological in nature, meaning it relates to the way the brain processes certain kinds of information — particularly the sounds within words, verbal memory and the speed at which written language is handled. Dyslexia varies from person to person and ranges from mild to more marked.

  • A specific learning difficulty: it affects particular skills, chiefly reading, writing and spelling, rather than general ability.
  • Lifelong: dyslexia does not go away, though people learn strategies that help them manage it well.
  • Individual: no two people experience dyslexia in exactly the same way.

A difference, not an illness

Dyslexia is one of the most common specific learning difficulties, and many of the people you support — and colleagues you work alongside — may have it, whether or not it has ever been formally identified. It is a difference in how a person processes written language, not an illness to be cured and not a sign of laziness or lack of effort. People with dyslexia often work harder than others to achieve the same result with reading and writing.

This course raises your awareness so that you can recognise dyslexia, understand its impact, and support people with dignity and the right reasonable adjustments. It is knowledge-level learning only. It does not replace a formal dyslexia assessment carried out by a qualified professional, and it is not a substitute for supervised, hands-on training.

Dyslexia and intelligence

One of the most important things to understand is that dyslexia is not linked to a person’s intelligence. People with dyslexia are found across the whole range of intellectual ability, from those who struggle in many areas to those who are highly able. A person may have a rich vocabulary, strong reasoning and plenty of ideas, yet still find reading and spelling difficult. Confusing dyslexia with low ability is both inaccurate and hurtful.

  • Across all abilities: dyslexia occurs in people of every level of intelligence.
  • Not a measure of ability: difficulty with reading or spelling says nothing about how clever a person is.
  • Often a mismatch: a person’s spoken ideas may far outstrip what they can easily put on paper.

Difficulties that often occur together

Dyslexia frequently occurs alongside other specific learning difficulties and related conditions. It commonly overlaps with dyspraxia, which affects co-ordination and movement, and with dyscalculia, which affects skills with numbers. It can also co-occur with attention difficulties such as ADHD. Because these difficulties often appear together, a person may face a mix of challenges rather than a single one.

Knowing this helps you avoid narrow assumptions. If someone finds reading hard, they may also find organisation, co-ordination or numbers challenging — or they may not. Each person is different, so notice the individual in front of you rather than expecting a fixed pattern.

items.question: Which statement about dyslexia and intelligence is accurate?

answers.text: Dyslexia is not linked to intelligence and occurs in people across the whole range of ability.
answers.feedback: Correct. Dyslexia is found in people of every level of ability and says nothing about how clever a person is.

answers.text: Dyslexia only affects people who have a low level of general ability.
answers.feedback: Not so. Dyslexia occurs across the full range of intelligence, including in highly able people.

answers.text: A person with dyslexia cannot have strong reasoning or a wide vocabulary.
answers.feedback: Many people with dyslexia reason well and are articulate. Reading difficulty does not limit ability.

answers.text: Difficulty with spelling is a reliable sign that someone is not clever.
answers.feedback: Spelling difficulty is a feature of dyslexia, not a measure of intelligence.

items.html:
Your role as a care worker

You are not expected to diagnose dyslexia or to teach reading. Your role is to be aware, understanding and practical — to recognise when someone may find written information difficult, to adapt how you communicate, and to make the reasonable adjustments that help a person take part fully and with dignity. Small changes in your everyday practice can make a real difference to someone’s confidence and independence.

  • Be aware: notice when reading, writing or organisation seem to cause difficulty.
  • Adapt: change how you share information so it is easier to understand.
  • Support dignity: offer help in a way that never embarrasses or singles a person out.

Working within your limits

Understanding the boundaries of your role protects both you and the people you support. Formally identifying dyslexia is the job of qualified professionals, such as specialist teachers or educational psychologists, through a proper assessment. Your part is to spot when someone is struggling, respond helpfully, and signpost towards assessment and support where that is wanted.

  • Do not diagnose: never label someone as dyslexic yourself.
  • Do make adjustments: you can and should adapt communication straight away, without waiting for any diagnosis.
  • Do signpost: point people towards proper assessment and support when they wish to explore it.

items.question: You should wait for a formal diagnosis of dyslexia before making any reasonable adjustments to how you share information.
items.correctMsg: Correct. You do not need a diagnosis to adapt your communication. Helpful adjustments can and should be made straight away.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. You never need to wait for a diagnosis to make helpful adjustments — adapt your communication whenever it helps.

items.html:
Common signs in reading and writing

Dyslexia shows itself in different ways, but there are some common signs you may notice. In reading, a person may read slowly and with effort, lose their place, or avoid reading aloud. In writing and spelling, they may spell the same word several different ways, muddle the order of letters, or produce writing that does not reflect how well they can express themselves out loud.

  • Reading: slow, effortful reading, losing the place, or avoiding reading in front of others.
  • Spelling: inconsistent spelling and letters written in the wrong order.
  • Writing: written work that does not match a person’s clear spoken ability.

Memory, organisation and time

Dyslexia is not only about reading and writing. Many people also find aspects of memory, organisation and time challenging. Someone might struggle to remember a sequence of spoken instructions, mix up appointments, or find it hard to keep track of belongings and paperwork. Telling left from right, or judging how long a task will take, can also be difficult.

These signs are easy to misread as carelessness or not paying attention. Recognising them as part of dyslexia helps you respond with patience and practical help rather than frustration, and reminds you to check understanding rather than assume someone was simply not listening.

items.question: A person you support often loses their place when reading, spells the same word several ways, and struggles to remember a list of spoken instructions. These are:

answers.text: Common signs associated with dyslexia, which affects reading, spelling and verbal memory.
answers.feedback: Correct. These are recognised signs of dyslexia and a reason to offer patient, practical support.

answers.text: A clear sign that the person is not paying attention or making an effort.
answers.feedback: These signs are easily misread as carelessness. They are more likely to reflect dyslexia.

answers.text: Proof that the person has a low level of intelligence.
answers.feedback: Dyslexia is not linked to intelligence. These signs say nothing about a person’s ability.

answers.text: Signs that should be ignored, as they have nothing to do with how you communicate.
answers.feedback: These signs are exactly when your communication should adapt to help the person.

items.html:
Visual stress

Some people with dyslexia also experience visual stress, sometimes called Meares-Irlen syndrome. This is a separate difficulty that can occur alongside dyslexia, where reading brings on discomfort or distortion. Words may appear to blur, move, flicker or run together, and bright white backgrounds or the glare of a screen can make it worse. Visual stress can make reading tiring and uncomfortable even for people who are not dyslexic.

  • Distorted text: letters and words may seem to blur, swim or jump on the page.
  • Glare sensitivity: bright white paper or screens can increase discomfort.
  • A separate condition: visual stress can occur with or without dyslexia.

The emotional impact

Living with dyslexia can take an emotional toll, especially when it has gone unrecognised or a person has been made to feel that they are simply not trying hard enough. Years of difficulty with reading and writing can knock a person’s confidence and self-esteem, and may lead to anxiety, frustration or embarrassment in situations that involve paperwork or reading aloud. Some people go to great lengths to hide their difficulties.

Being aware of this hidden impact helps you support people kindly. A calm, accepting manner, and never making a person feel foolish, allows them to ask for help without shame.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: Some people experience visual *stress*, where words appear to blur or move on the page. Years of difficulty can also affect a person’s *confidence*, and may lead to *anxiety* or low *self-esteem*.

items.html:
Strengths often seen

Dyslexia is a difference, not simply a set of difficulties, and many people with dyslexia have real strengths. While experiences vary from person to person, people with dyslexia often show creativity, strong problem-solving, and an ability to see the bigger picture or think in three dimensions. Many are articulate, good with people, and able to find original solutions that others miss.

  • Creative thinking: fresh ideas and original approaches to problems.
  • Big-picture skills: seeing how things connect and spotting patterns.
  • People and practical skills: strong verbal, social or hands-on abilities.

Recognising the whole person

Focusing only on what a person finds hard paints an unfair and incomplete picture. When you notice and value someone’s strengths, you help build the confidence that difficulties may have worn away. This matters for the people you support and for colleagues alike.

A strengths-based approach means shaping tasks so a person can use what they are good at, while offering support where they need it. Someone who finds forms difficult may be wonderful at talking with people, calming a situation or thinking on their feet. Seeing the whole person is at the heart of good, inclusive care.

items.question: Which of the following best reflects a fair, awareness-level view of dyslexia?

answers.text: Dyslexia brings difficulties with reading and writing, but people with dyslexia often have real strengths too.
answers.feedback: Correct. A fair view recognises both the difficulties and the genuine strengths people often have.

answers.text: Dyslexia is only a list of weaknesses and brings no strengths at all.
answers.feedback: Dyslexia is a difference, not only a difficulty. Many people show real creativity and problem-solving.

answers.text: People with dyslexia have nothing to offer in a care setting.
answers.feedback: This is untrue and demeaning. People with dyslexia bring many valuable strengths.

answers.text: Strengths should be ignored so that all attention goes on the difficulties.
answers.feedback: Ignoring strengths gives an unfair picture. A strengths-based approach builds confidence.

items.html:
The Equality Act 2010

The Equality Act 2010 protects people from discrimination. Under the Act, a person is considered to have a disability if they have a physical or mental impairment that has a substantial and long-term adverse effect on their ability to carry out normal day-to-day activities. Dyslexia can meet this definition, which means a person with dyslexia may be protected as a disabled person under the law.

  • Protection from discrimination: the Act makes it unlawful to treat someone unfairly because of a disability.
  • Dyslexia may count: where it has a substantial, long-term effect, dyslexia can be a disability in law.
  • Applies widely: the Act covers services, workplaces and education.

The duty to make reasonable adjustments

The Equality Act places a duty on employers and service providers to make reasonable adjustments so that disabled people are not put at a disadvantage. A reasonable adjustment is a sensible change that removes or reduces a barrier — for example, providing information in an easy-read format, allowing extra time, or giving instructions verbally as well as in writing.

For your work, this means that adapting how you communicate is not just kindness — it can be part of meeting a legal duty. What counts as reasonable depends on the situation, but the aim is always the same: to give a person with dyslexia fair and equal access to care, information and opportunities.

items.question: Under the Equality Act 2010, what is a reasonable adjustment?

answers.text: A sensible change that removes or reduces a barrier so a disabled person is not put at a disadvantage.
answers.feedback: Correct. Reasonable adjustments remove barriers so people are not disadvantaged.

answers.text: A special favour that service providers may offer but never have any duty to make.
answers.feedback: The Act places a duty to make reasonable adjustments — it is not merely optional kindness.

answers.text: A change that only ever applies in schools and never in care settings.
answers.feedback: The duty applies widely, including in services and workplaces such as care settings.

answers.text: Proof that a person has been formally diagnosed before anything can be adapted.
answers.feedback: You do not need a diagnosis to make helpful adjustments to your communication.

items.html:
Easy read and clear information

One of the most useful adjustments is to present information clearly. Easy-read materials use simple words, short sentences and supporting images to make meaning easy to follow. Even where full easy-read is not available, you can apply the same principles: plain language, one idea at a time, and a clear layout with headings and bullet points rather than dense blocks of text.

  • Plain words: choose everyday language and explain anything unavoidable.
  • Short and simple: keep sentences short and cover one point at a time.
  • Support with images: pictures and symbols can carry meaning alongside words.

Presentation that helps

How text looks on the page or screen matters as much as the words themselves. Small changes in presentation can make reading far less tiring for someone with dyslexia or visual stress. A clean, uncluttered layout with generous spacing is easier on the eye than cramped text.

  • Readable fonts: use a clear, plain font at a comfortable size, and avoid italics or block capitals for long passages.
  • Off-white backgrounds: a cream or pastel background reduces glare compared with bright white.
  • Space it out: use good line spacing, clear headings and bullet points to break up information.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap.
items.textField: Easy-read information uses simple *words*, short *sentences* and supporting *images* to make meaning clear. Presenting information in this way is a reasonable *adjustment*.

items.html:
Overlays, extra time and verbal instructions

A range of simple, practical adjustments can help a person with dyslexia. Coloured overlays — tinted transparent sheets placed over a page — or coloured backgrounds and paper can reduce visual stress and make text steadier to read. Giving people extra time removes the pressure that makes reading and writing harder, and offering instructions verbally as well as in writing means a person is not relying on text alone.

  • Colour: coloured overlays, paper or screen tints can ease visual stress for some people.
  • Extra time: allowing longer for reading, forms or tasks reduces pressure.
  • Say it too: give key information verbally as well as in writing.

Assistive technology

Technology offers powerful support and is often built into the phones, tablets and computers people already use. Text-to-speech software reads written text aloud, while speech-to-text lets a person dictate rather than type. Spellcheckers, audio recordings and reminder apps all help reduce the load on reading, writing and memory.

You do not need to be a technology expert. Simply being aware that these tools exist means you can encourage their use and help a person access support that suits them. Where someone already uses a particular tool or method, respect it and build it into how you work together.

items.question: A person you support finds reading forms tiring and stressful. Which of the following are helpful reasonable adjustments?

answers.text: Offering a coloured overlay, allowing extra time, and going through the form verbally as well.
answers.feedback: Correct. These simple adjustments reduce pressure and make the information easier to access.

answers.text: Insisting they read the whole form quickly and unaided to save time.
answers.feedback: Rushing a person adds pressure and makes reading harder. Allow time and offer support.

answers.text: Telling them that adjustments are unnecessary because everyone finds forms hard.
answers.feedback: Dismissing the difficulty denies a person fair access. Reasonable adjustments are appropriate here.

answers.text: Refusing to help until they have proved they have a diagnosis.
answers.feedback: You never need a diagnosis to make helpful adjustments. Offer support straight away.

items.html:
Breaking tasks down

Large or multi-step tasks can feel overwhelming, particularly where memory and organisation are affected. Breaking a task into smaller, clear steps makes it far more manageable and reduces the chance of something being missed. Giving one instruction at a time, and checking understanding before moving on, helps a person keep track without feeling lost or rushed.

  • Small steps: break tasks into clear, single steps rather than one long list.
  • One at a time: give instructions one by one and allow time to take each in.
  • Check in: gently confirm understanding before moving on, without testing the person.

Avoiding putting people on the spot

Being asked to read aloud, fill in a form in front of others, or answer quickly can be deeply uncomfortable for someone with dyslexia. Putting a person on the spot can trigger anxiety and embarrassment, and may cause them to avoid situations altogether. Thoughtful care means never singling someone out or making them perform under pressure.

  • Never force reading aloud: do not expect anyone to read in front of others unless they choose to.
  • Offer privacy: help with forms or paperwork discreetly, away from an audience.
  • Give warning and time: let people prepare rather than springing tasks on them.

These small courtesies protect a person’s dignity and make it far easier for them to accept the help they need.

Supporting people and colleagues

Inclusive practice means thinking about everyone around you, not only the people you directly support. A colleague may have dyslexia too, and the same understanding and adjustments that help someone using your service can help a member of the team. Whether it is a person you care for or a co-worker, the principles are the same: understand, adapt and treat people with respect.

  • People you support: adapt communication and offer reasonable adjustments as part of everyday care.
  • Colleagues: be considerate about written handovers, rotas and instructions.
  • Shared principles: understanding and respect apply to everyone.

Person-centred approaches

There is no single right way to support someone with dyslexia, because every person is different. A person-centred approach means asking the individual what helps them, listening to their preferences, and shaping support around their needs rather than applying a fixed formula. The person themselves is the expert in what works for them.

This might mean using audio instead of written notes for one person, coloured paper for another, or simply more time and a patient manner. By involving the person in decisions, you offer support that genuinely fits and that respects their independence and choice.

items.question: Reasonable adjustments and understanding for dyslexia are only relevant for the people you support, never for colleagues.
items.correctMsg: Correct. Colleagues may have dyslexia too. Inclusive practice means considering everyone, including members of your team.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. Colleagues can have dyslexia as well, so understanding and adjustments matter for the whole team.

items.html:
Confidentiality and respect

Information about a person’s dyslexia is personal, and it should be treated with the same care as any other confidential information. Some people are open about being dyslexic, while others prefer to keep it private, perhaps because of past experiences. It is not for you to share this information with others without good reason and the person’s agreement.

  • Keep it confidential: share information only with those who need it and, wherever possible, with the person’s consent.
  • Respect privacy: let people decide for themselves how open to be about their dyslexia.
  • Record appropriately: note the support a person needs in the care plan, following your organisation’s policies.

Not making assumptions

Dyslexia affects everyone differently, so assumptions can easily lead you astray. Do not assume that a person cannot read at all, that they will struggle with everything, or that finding one thing hard means they find everything hard. Equally, do not assume that someone who reads slowly is careless or not trying. Assumptions can be both inaccurate and demeaning.

The safest approach is to ask, listen and check. Find out what the person can do, what they find difficult, and how they would like to be supported. Treating each person as an individual, rather than as a label, keeps your care respectful, accurate and genuinely helpful.

Signposting to assessment and support

Some people may wish to explore a formal assessment, whether to understand themselves better or to access particular support. A formal diagnosis is made by qualified professionals, such as specialist assessors or educational psychologists. Organisations such as the British Dyslexia Association offer information, guidance and helplines for people who want to learn more.

  • Formal assessment: carried out by qualified specialists, not by care workers.
  • Trusted information: the British Dyslexia Association is a well-known source of guidance and support.
  • Follow the person’s lead: offer signposting when it is wanted, without pressure.

Putting awareness into practice

Supporting people with dyslexia rarely calls for anything dramatic. It is the everyday habits that count: sharing information clearly, allowing time, offering practical adjustments, protecting people’s dignity and treating each person as an individual. When you build these into your routine, you help people with dyslexia take part fully, confidently and on equal terms.

If someone wants to explore assessment or support, encourage them gently and point them towards trusted sources such as their GP or the British Dyslexia Association. Record the support a person needs so that others can follow it consistently.

This course has raised your awareness of dyslexia and how to support people with dignity and reasonable adjustments. It is knowledge-level learning only. It does not replace a formal dyslexia assessment carried out by a qualified professional, and it is not a substitute for supervised, hands-on training.

items.question: Someone you support says they think they may be dyslexic and asks what they can do. What is the most appropriate response?

answers.text: Listen, reassure them, and gently signpost to trusted sources such as their GP or the British Dyslexia Association.
answers.feedback: Correct. Listening and signposting to trusted sources is exactly the right, in-role response.

answers.text: Tell them confidently that you can diagnose their dyslexia yourself.
answers.feedback: Diagnosis is for qualified professionals. Your role is to support and signpost, not to diagnose.

answers.text: Say there is no point looking into it because nothing can be done.
answers.feedback: This is discouraging and untrue. Assessment and support can genuinely help.

answers.text: Share their concern with other people without asking them first.
answers.feedback: This breaches confidentiality. Respect the person’s privacy and their choice about who to tell.
